text: 0.3g of fat. omega 3 is doing a lot of the work here. for a vegetable, nutrient payoff is the whole point.
9g of carbs. fibre is doing a lot of the work here. for vegetables, low downside is useful, but there still needs to be real payoff.
3.4g of protein. bioavailability is one of the best parts of the protein story. protein is not the main pitch here, but extra support still matters.
vitamin k at 148% daily value, vitamin c at 94% daily value, vitamin a at 15% daily value stand out most. that is major vitamin support.
potassium at 11% daily value stands out most. useful, but still pretty limited.

pros first: makes meals feel more balanced. the category has real value beyond single-nutrient stats. good volume for the calories. that matters for satiety and general usefulness. very low downside whole food. vegetables win a lot simply by offering clean nutritional payoff.

the drawbacks next: taste and texture can limit compliance. a strong paper profile still needs real-world repeatability. energy density is very low. good for some use cases, but not enough on its own. usually needs pairing to make a full meal. most vegetables are not complete by themselves.
S tier. Best when you want low downside and easy micronutrient support.
